serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of fresh raspberries is approximately one cup (123 g), commonly consumed as a snack, added to yogurt, cereal, or used as a topping for desserts.

nutrient_source.title: Raspberries, raw — USDA FoodData Central, FDC ID 167755 (SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record sourced from HPB Singapore. Core macros and micronutrients cross-referenced and confirmed against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for raw raspberries (FDC ID 167755). Values are consistent. USDA used as primary approved source for this generic Western fruit. HPB values retained where consistent; USDA used to supplement saturated fat, potassium, vitamin C, and other micronutrients not reported by HPB.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Raspberry GI of 25 (low) is reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and corroborated by Atkinson et al. 2008 International Tables of Glycemic Index. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (25 × 11.94 × 1.23) / 100 ≈ 3.7; rounded to 2.5 for a standard 100 g reference portion (GL = 25 × 11.94 / 100 ≈ 3.0; using 123 g serving: 25 × 14.69 / 100 ≈ 3.7). GL per serving (123 g) = 25 × (11.94 × 1.23) / 100 = 25 × 14.69 / 100 = 3.67, reported as 3.7. Note: gi_category confirmed low (GI < 55).

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (1 mg/100 g) and meaningful potassium (151 mg/100 g) with high dietary fibre (6.5 g/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Raspberries are an excellent choice for individuals with hypertension: sodium is negligible at 1 mg/100 g, potassium at 151 mg/100 g supports vasodilation, and high dietary fibre at 6.5 g/100 g contributes to blood pressure reduction consistent with DASH diet principles.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (GI 25), low sugar (4.42 g/100 g), and very high dietary fibre (6.5 g/100 g) slowing glucose absorption
type2_diabetes.guidance: Raspberries are well-suited for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes: their low GI of 25 and exceptionally high fibre content of 6.5 g/100 g significantly attenuate postprandial glucose response, and their low sugar content of 4.42 g/100 g supports glycaemic control consistent with ADA dietary recommendations.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat (0.02 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, high dietary fibre (6.5 g/100 g), and rich polyphenol content (anthocyanins, ellagic acid)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Raspberries support cardiovascular health through negligible saturated fat (0.02 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, high soluble fibre contributing to LDL reduction, and abundant anthocyanins and ellagic acid with demonstrated anti-inflammatory and endothelial-protective effects per ACC/AHA dietary guidance.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High polyphenol content (anthocyanins, ellagic acid) and vitamin C (26.2 mg/100 g) with anti-inflammatory properties
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Raspberries are beneficial for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to their rich anthocyanin and ellagic acid content providing antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects, and vitamin C at 26.2 mg/100 g supporting collagen synthesis, consistent with EULAR dietary guidance for inflammatory arthritis.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Raspberry (Rubus idaeus) is a small, soft, red berry fruit belonging to the rose family (Rosaceae), widely cultivated across Europe, North America, and temperate regions globally. Fresh raspberries have a delicate, aggregate drupe structure composed of many small drupelets arranged around a central core. Their flavour profile is characteristically sweet-tart when fully ripe, with a fragrant aroma and a soft, juicy texture that makes them highly perishable. Raspberries are consumed raw as a fresh snack, used in desserts, smoothies, jams, yogurt toppings, and baked goods, or processed into juices and preserves. Nutritionally, raspberries are notable for their exceptionally high dietary fibre content of 6.5 g per 100 g — one of the highest among common fruits — which supports digestive health, glycaemic control, and cardiovascular protection. They provide 52 kcal per 100 g with modest protein (1.2 g), very low fat (0.65 g), and moderate carbohydrates (11.94 g) with only 4.42 g of sugar. Their glycemic index is low at 25, making them suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or metabolic syndrome. Raspberries are rich in polyphenols including anthocyanins and ellagic acid, which confer antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties relevant to cardiovascular disease and rheumatoid arthritis management. Vitamin C at 26.2 mg/100 g supports immune function and collagen synthesis. Sodium is negligible at 1 mg/100 g, making raspberries fully compatible with DASH dietary patterns for hypertension management. Potassium at 151 mg/100 g further supports vascular health. Raspberries are naturally vegan, gluten-free, dairy-free, halal, and kosher.
common_user_queries: Can I eat raspberries if I have high blood pressure?
What is the glycemic index of raspberries?
Are raspberries suitable for a low-sugar diet?
How much sugar is in raspberries?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced from HPB Singapore (confidence 0.95) and cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 167755) for raw raspberries. Values are consistent across both approved sources. GI of 25 confirmed via University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 peer-reviewed publication. Micronutrient values supplemented from USDA SR Legacy. No estimation or analogy required for core nutritional data.
notes: 1. Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, iron) sourced from HPB Singapore and confirmed consistent with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 167755). 2. Saturated fat (0.02 g), monounsaturated fat (0.06 g), polyunsaturated fat (0.38 g), trans fat (0.0 g), potassium (151 mg), vitamin C (26.2 mg), vitamin A (2 mcg RAE), B-vitamins, vitamin D (0.0 mcg), vitamin E (0.87 mg), phosphorus (29 mg), magnesium (22 mg), zinc (0.42 mg), selenium (0.2 mcg), cholesterol (0.0 mg), and water (85.75 g) sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 167755. 3. Vitamin B12 is 0.0 mcg and vitamin D is 0.0 mcg: confirmed zero for plant-based raw fruit per USDA SR Legacy — raspberries contain no animal-derived components and thus naturally contain no vitamin B12; vitamin D is naturally absent in this fruit. Trans fat is 0.0 g: confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy for raw raspberries. Cholesterol is 0.0 mg: naturally zero as this is a plant food. 4. Glycemic load per serving (123 g) calculated as: GL = GI × (carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = 25 × (11.94 × 1.23) / 100 = 25 × 14.69 / 100 ≈ 3.7. The gi_source notes field contains the full calculation. The glycemic_load_per_serving field is set to 3.7 for the 123 g serving. 5. Dietary tag 'wholegrain' from input cultural.dietary_tags has been removed as it is not applicable to fresh fruit. 6. No mandatory multilingual names required as raspberry is a generic Western fruit, not a Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese dish. 7. Local_names fields are null as no cuisine-specific mandatory language requirement applies. 8. FoodOn URI, FoodEx2 code, and SNOMED CT code are defensible standard ontology anchors for raspberry as a raw fruit commodity.
